skill.name: literature-statistics
skill.description: Generate standardized publication-year and journal distribution tables from local references (BibTeX, RIS, plain text) or PDF files. Operates entirely offline with no network calls.
skill.summary: Parse local reference files and PDFs to produce publication year and journal distribution tables for systematic reviews and literature surveys.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: After evaluating all 60 static analysis findings against the actual source code, the vast majority (57/60) are false positives caused by pattern-matching limitations. The scanner confused Markdown code fence backticks (```) with Ruby shell execution backticks (42 findings), misidentified BibTeX example data and argparse initialization as weak cryptographic algorithms (7 findings), and misread documentation file extensions as hidden file access (3 findings). The 6 system reconnaissance findings in the audit JSON are also false positives. The only valid finding is a hardcoded source repository URL in documentation which is low risk. This skill is well-documented with clear safety constraints, no network exfiltration paths, no credential access, and no malicious intent. The existing security audit (literature-statistics_audit_result_v2.json) confirms security gate PASS and Production Ready grade.

medium_findings.title: Documented Shell Command Execution
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md documents multiple shell commands for running Python scripts (process_pdfs.py). The AI executing this skill could be prompted to run these commands with user-provided paths. Commands use argparse for input validation and are restricted to documented arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands use argparse with required flags and no user-injection vectors. The script validates input directory existence before processing. Risk is moderate if user paths are unsanitized, but the skill explicitly bounds file access to user-provided paths.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source Repository URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL to the GitHub source repository (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). This is a legitimate source attribution link, not used for data exfiltration or malicious callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URL is clearly a source repository link in the attribution header. The skill explicitly states 'no network access; no external APIs' throughout the documentation, confirming this is static attribution only.

low_findings.title: PDF Filesystem Read Access
low_findings.description: The process_pdfs.py script reads PDF files from user-specified directories using pypdf. Filesystem access is bounded to the --input-dir argument and validates the directory exists before scanning. The skill documentation explicitly states 'Do not read files outside the user-provided paths.'
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear filesystem read operations confirmed in source code. All access is user-directed through --input-dir with path validation. Intent is legitimate and well-documented for the skill's stated purpose.

content.user_title: Generate Publication Statistics from Local References
content.value_statement: Researchers waste hours manually counting publication years and journal distributions for literature reviews. This skill parses BibTeX, RIS, plain text citations, and PDF files to produce standardized year and journal distribution tables with counts and percentages, all without any network access.
actual_capabilities: Parse BibTeX, RIS, EndNote, and plain text citation formats to extract year and journal metadata
Extract year and journal information from PDF metadata and first-page text content
Generate publication year distribution tables with counts and percentages
Generate journal distribution tables with optional top-N filtering
Produce structured Markdown output with summary statistics including total items and unknown-field counts
Normalize journal names using configurable rules (trim, title case, trailing punctuation removal)
limitations: Cannot access external databases or APIs for citation lookup or metadata enrichment
PDF year/journal extraction is best-effort and may produce unknown values for scanned documents
Journal normalization does not expand abbreviations or resolve name variants
Does not support network-based reference managers or cloud file storage

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Year Distribution
use_cases.description: A PhD candidate compiling a systematic review needs to show the publication year range of included studies. They paste their BibTeX library and receive a year distribution table showing which years are represented.

use_cases.title: Journal Portfolio Analysis
use_cases.description: A research group lead wants to understand which journals their team publishes in most frequently. They provide a folder of reference files and receive a journal distribution table ranked by publication count.
use_cases.target_user: Research group leader or lab manager

use_cases.title: PDF Collection Metadata Survey
use_cases.description: A librarian has a folder of PDF articles from a conference and needs a quick overview of publication years and venues. They run the PDF script against the folder and get summary statistics without manual inspection.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Year Distribution from Pasted Citations
prompt_templates.prompt:
Generate a publication year distribution from the following citations. Use the literature-statistics skill to extract years and produce a table with year, count, and percentage columns.

[Paste your BibTeX, RIS, or plain text citations here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick analysis of a small batch of references from copy-pasted text

prompt_templates.title: Journal Distribution from Reference File
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the reference file at [file_path] and generate a journal distribution table using the literature-statistics skill. Show counts, percentages, and sort by count descending. Include a summary with total items and unknown journal count.
prompt_templates.scenario: Analysis of journal spread from a local BibTeX or RIS file

prompt_templates.title: Full PDF Directory Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the literature-statistics PDF processing script on the directory [path/to/pdfs] with recursive scanning. Produce both year distribution and journal distribution tables. Flag any PDFs where metadata could not be read and explain why.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive analysis of a PDF collection with error reporting

prompt_templates.title: Mixed Input Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have two sets of references: one from PubMed (file: pubmed_results.txt) and one from Scopus (file: scopus_results.bib). Use the literature-statistics skill to process each file separately and compare the year and journal distributions between the two datasets in a single report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing publication patterns across multiple literature databases

output_examples.input: Paste BibTeX and RIS citations for a literature review chapter
Run process_pdfs.py on a folder of conference PDFs

best_practices: Validate that all input reference files are accessible and formatted correctly before running the analysis to avoid unknown-field counts
Use the --recursive flag when analyzing PDF collections spread across subdirectories to ensure complete coverage
Review the unknown year and unknown journal counts in the summary output to assess data completeness and identify problematic citations
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to fabricate or guess missing citation metadata - ambiguous items must be reported as unknown
Do not attempt network lookups or API calls to enrich citation data; the skill is designed for offline use only
Do not modify the PDF script to access files outside the specified input directory or to make network requests

faq.question: What citation formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports BibTeX, RIS, EndNote, and plain text citation formats. It also processes PDF files using a Python script for metadata and first-page text extraction.

faq.question: Does this skill access the internet or external databases?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed for local-only operation. It does not make any network calls, access external APIs, or use credentials. The only URL in the documentation is a reference link to the source repository.

faq.question: How accurate is the PDF year and journal extraction?
faq.answer: Extraction is best-effort. For PDFs with proper metadata tags, extraction is reliable. For scanned documents or PDFs without metadata, the script falls back to scanning the first page text. Ambiguous or missing values are reported as unknown in the summary.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with reference managers like Zotero or Mendeley?
faq.answer: Yes, you can export references from Zotero or Mendeley in BibTeX or RIS format and use those exported files as input. The skill does not integrate directly with reference manager APIs.

faq.question: What is the difference between unknown and ambiguous values?
faq.answer: Unknown means no year or journal information was found. Ambiguous means multiple different values were found for the same citation (for example, different years in PDF metadata vs first-page text), and the first detected value was used.

faq.question: How are journal names normalized?
faq.answer: Journal names are trimmed of whitespace, trailing punctuation is removed, spacing is collapsed, and inconsistent casing is converted to Title Case. Abbreviations are not expanded and name variants are not merged.
